coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence biosensor based on gold-carbon dot probe for efficient detection of cholesterol
coredata.dc:creator: Barua, Shaswat
Gogoi, Satyabrat
Khan, Raju
coredata.dc:description: Cardiovascular diseases have raised serious concerns in recent times with a huge number of mortality all over the world. Herein, we report a fluorescence probe, comprising of carbon quantum dots (CD) and gold nanoparticles (AuNP) for efficient sensing of cholesterol, the most important cardio-risk-marker. A simple hydrothermal method has been employed to synthesize the gold-carbon-dot nanohybrid (AuCD). Cholesterol oxidase immobilized AuCD (Ch-AuCD) showed prominent fluorescence emission at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) has been observed between Ch-AuCD and graphene oxide with visible fluorescence intensity turn off. This has been recovered by the addition of a range of concentrations of cholesterol which turn on the fluorescence intensity. This ‘on-off’ mechanism could efficiently detect cholesterol within the concentration range of 10–100 nM (lower detection limit ∼10 nM). Moreover, selectivity of the probe towards cholesterol has been ascertained by analyzing a range of interfering analytes, viz. ascorbic acid, urea, glucose, galactose and L-cysteine. Thus, we forward this FRET based ‘nano-switch’ with high selectivity and sensitivity for efficient sensing of cholesterol. Further, ease of detection endorses this fluorescence-based method for real sample analysis, after scrutinizing the allied factors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cholesterol
Fluorescence
Biosensor
Carbon dot
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ch-AuCD as fluorescence probe for cholesterol sensing
Structural and morphological analysis of Ch-AuCD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Rajasthan
Department of Biotechnology , Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Director, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat is deeply acknowledged for his kind support. SB thankfully acknowledges CSIR , India for CSIR Nehru Post Doctoral Research Fellowship (HRDG/CSIR-Nehru PDF/CS/EMR-1/01/2017). SG acknowledges SERB , DST , India for financial support through grant no. PDF/2016/003142 . RK acknowledges DBT , India for financial support through Grant No. BT/PR16223/NER/95/494/2016 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of AuCD by hydrothermal method, taking D-(+)-glucose and HAuCl4 as precursors and immobilization of ChOx, by physical adsorption technique.
(a) UV–vis spectra, showing characteristic peaks for AuNP, CD and AuCD and (b) X-ray diffractogram of AuCD and CD.
SEM micrographs of CD (a, b) and AuCD (c, d) at different magnifications.
TEM micrographs of (a) AuCD (inset SAED pattern of CD) (b) AuNP (inset SAED pattern AuNP), (c) AuNP surrounded by CD and (d) AuNP in close proximity with CD (inset HRTEM image).
(a) Fluorescence quenching of the Ch-AuCD with varying concentrations of GO and (b) Calibration curve for turn-off fluorescence intensity of Ch-AuCD by GO.
(a) Turn-on fluorescence of Ch-AuCD/GO by the addition of cholesterol (10–100 nM) and (b) Calibration curve for cholesterol sensing.
Plausible mechanism of detection of cholesterol by Ch-AuCD probe.
Comparison of fluorescence responses of Ch-AuCD sensor towards cholesterol and other interferents.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence biosensor based on gold-carbon dot probe for efficient detection of cholesterol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Group, Chemical Sciences & Technology Division, Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, CSIR North-East Institute of Science & Technology, 785006, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research
CSIR North-East Institute of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Basic Sciences, Assam Kaziranga University, Koraikhowa, NH-7, Jorhat, 785006, Assam, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cardiovascular diseases have raised serious concerns in recent times with a huge number of mortality all over the world. Herein, we report a fluorescence probe, comprising of carbon quantum dots (CD) and gold nanoparticles (AuNP) for efficient sensing of cholesterol, the most important cardio-risk-marker. A simple hydrothermal method has been employed to synthesize the gold-carbon-dot nanohybrid (AuCD). Cholesterol oxidase immobilized AuCD (Ch-AuCD) showed prominent fluorescence emission at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) has been observed between Ch-AuCD and graphene oxide with visible fluorescence intensity turn off. This has been recovered by the addition of a range of concentrations of cholesterol which turn on the fluorescence intensity. This ‘on-off’ mechanism could efficiently detect cholesterol within the concentration range of 10–100 nM (lower detection limit ∼10 nM). Moreover, selectivity of the probe towards cholesterol has been ascertained by analyzing a range of interfering analytes, viz. ascorbic acid, urea, glucose, galactose and L-cysteine. Thus, we forward this FRET based ‘nano-switch’ with high selectivity and sensitivity for efficient sensing of cholesterol. Further, ease of detection endorses this fluorescence-based method for real sample analysis, after scrutinizing the allied factors.

ce:section.ce:para: Observing the worldwide rate of heart diseases, the periodic monitoring of cardiovascular risk markers is inevitable. Plasma cholesterol, being one of the most prominent heart disease risk marker has raised serious concern [1]. Low density lipoprotein (LDL), the so-called bad cholesterol, beyond the normal range initiates pulmonary or coronary blockage and subsequent cardiac strokes [2–5]. So, a rapid, accurate and selective sensor for cholesterol is highly desired. Recent advances in biosensors confront issues like selectivity, sensitivity and ease of handling [6]. Thus, development of advanced biosensors demand the incorporation of these attributes. Besides the conventional spectrophotometric techniques, extensive research has been carried out for electrochemical detection of cholesterol [7–10]. However, low selectivity, tedious and expensive sensing processes restrict their commercial viability [11]. Saxena and Das recently reviewed the potentiality of nanomaterials in the fabrication of cholesterol biosensors. In this review, they have shown the variation of selectivity, sensitivity and detection limit of the biosensors, based on the electronic, optical and catalytic properties of the nanomaterials [12].

Fluorescence based biosensors have been delved into recently for efficient sensing of a range of bio-analytes [13–15]. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism has been employed in the domain of biosensors for micro-molar (μM) or nano-molar (nM) level detection of analytes [16,17]. Owing to the excellent fluorescence properties, nanoparticles and quantum dots have been explored for fabricating fluorimetric-biosensors [18,19]. Carbon-quantum dots (CD) are of great interest due to their cheap precursors and easy synthetic protocols [20]. Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behaviour of CD aids distinctive advantage in fluorescence based biosensing [21]. It is witnessed that on interaction with bio-analytes, semiconductor quantum dots and nanoparticles show fluorescence quenching, which can be used for ascertaining the biosensing ability of a nanomaterial.15] Gold nanoparticles (AuNP) have also been used in FRET based studies either as a fluorophore or as a quencher [14]. Again, two dimensional (2D) nanostructures like graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), MoS2 etc. have also been used as quenchers in FRET based fluorescence biosensing [13,22–25]. Herein, we demonstrate the development of a fluorescent probe by the combination of CD and AuNP (AuCD). Cholesterol oxidase (ChOx) has been immobilized onto the probe for selective sensing of cholesterol [26]. GO, being a strong fluorophore, has been utilized for turning-off the fluorescence intensity of cholesterol oxidase immobilized AuCD (Ch-AuCD), as FRET is very much probable between Ch-AuCD and GO. In a recent report, Gu et al. witnessed FRET between MoS2 and hyaluronic acid immobilized AuNP, which was recovered by micromolar addition of hyaluronidase [13].

Though some recent reports overrule the use of specific enzymes for cholesterol detection, still they could not address selectivity at very high sensitivity. Priyadarshini et al. reported a gold-carbon dot based nanoconjugate for enzyme free sensing of cholesterol with high selectivity. However, they could achieve sensitivity only up to milli molar level [15]. So, herein we have described the efficiency of a fluorescent probe based on Ch-AuCD for highly sensitive detection of cholesterol. Addition of cholesterol has been used to turn-on the fluorescence intensity, which ascertains the specific and selective sensing. Thus, we have put forward a ‘nano-switch’ that can be operated by on and off mechanism, based on FRET.

D-(+)-glucose (∼99.5% pure), gold(III) chloride (HAuCl4), sodium citrate tribasic dehydrate (SCT∼99.0% pure), graphene oxide (GO, 10 mg/mL, dispersion in water), cholesterol (∼92.5% pure) and cholesterol oxidase (ChOx, 100 U/mL from Streptomyces sp.) were procured from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Allied chemicals and reagents were purchased from, Merck, India and used without further purification.

AuCD was synthesized by a single step hydrothermal method. D-(+)-glucose (30 mg) was taken in 50 mL of water in a 100 mL conical flask. A solution of HAuCl4 (0.01 M) was prepared in water and added to glucose solution. The mixture was allowed to stir at room temperature for 30 min. Then, SCT (0.01 M) was added drop wise to the reaction vessel [27]. After the addition, the reaction mixture was poured into a Teflon lined hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a hot air oven. After the completion of the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature. The resultant product was AuCD with ruby-purple colour. Solid content of the solution was determined by drying 1 mL of the solution in a glass petri-dish.

ChOx (100 U/mL) was diluted 100 times in Milli-Q water and immobilized onto AuCD by physical adsorption technique [28]. Briefly, diluted ChOx solution (1 U/mL) was added to 1 mL of AuCD, taken in a 15 mL glass bottle and stirred for 5 h at 4 °C, followed by ultrasonication for 15 min. The immobilized nanohybrid system was encoded as Ch-AuCD.

UV-visible spectra were recorded in a SPECORD-200 spectrophotometer (SPECORD, Germany). X-ray diffraction patterns were analyzed by a Rigaku (Rigaku, Japan) X-ray diffractometer at the scanning speed of 2.0° min−1 (scanning range 2θ = 10°-60°). Morphology of the nanomaterials was studied by a field emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Sigma VP FE-SEM, Germany). Distribution pattern, morphological parameters and shape-size accords of AuCD were studied by a High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM, JEOL, JEMCXII, Japan) at an operating voltage of 200 kV.

The excitation dependent fluorescence emission of Ch-AuCD was studied in a Horiba, Fluorolog-3, USA, spectrofluorometer. Emission spectra were recorded in the excitation wavelengths range of 300–400 nm. Quantum yield was also determined by using an integrating sphere method. The same spectrofluorometer was used in various fluorescence based measurements for the detection of cholesterol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ch-AuCD as fluorescence probe for cholesterol sensing
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of Ch-AuCD (3 mg/mL) was taken in a 3 mL quartz cuvette, into which different concentrations of the quencher (GO) was added. Decrease in the fluorescent intensity of Ch-AuCD was recorded within the emission range of 380–700 nm, at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Amount of the quencher was optimized by recording the quenching pattern in the concentration range of 5–55 μg/mL (see results and discussion 0–55). A linearity calibration curve was plotted to elucidate the relationship between the fluorescence intensity turn-off and concentration of GO. The optimum concentration was calculated and was added to the working Ch-AuCD solution. A varying concentration (10–100 nM) of cholesterol was added to Ch-AuCD/GO to record the fluorescence intensity turn-on. Each reading was taken after incubating Ch-AuCD/GO with a particular concentration of cholesterol for 20 min at room temperature. Recovery of the quenched fluorescence intensity was measured within the same emission range as stated above. The relation between the turned-on fluorescence intensity and the concentration of cholesterol was determined by linearity-fit curve plotted in Origin 8.5 Pro software.

Selectivity of the sensor towards cholesterol was investigated in presence of interfering bio-analytes viz. ascorbic acid, urea, glucose, galactose and L-Cysteine [15]. Static fluorescence spectra of Ch-AuCD were recorded in presence of these biomolecules under similar experimental conditions. Same excitation wavelength and interfering analyte concentrations (50 nM) were maintained as that of cholesterol. If an analyte possesses interfering affect, it would enhance the fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, the inert analytes would leave the intensity unaltered. Thus, comparison of fluorescence intensities of the probe in presence interring elements and cholesterol provides the extent of selectivity of the sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of Ch-AuCD
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of quantum dots has attained great attention in recent decades owing to their immense potential in opto-electronic devices, biosensors, solar concentrators, single-photon devices and in vivo medical imaging [29]. Variation in the synthetic-protocol has a direct impact on the shape and size accords as well as the properties of the quantum dots [30]. Chemical and laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, microwave irradiation, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are the commonly employed techniques for synthesizing quantum dots [31]. However, hydrothermal method has been widely adopted for synthesising carbon based quantum dots [20]. De and Kark in 2013, proposed a mechanism for the formation of CD from a carbohydrate rich source, banana extract [32]. Accordingly in the present work, high temperature and pressure initiated the dehydration and decomposition of glucose, followed by condensation, aromatization and carbonization. Finally, nuclear burst of the resulted clusters yielded highly water soluble CD.

Again, synthesis of AuNP is primly carried out by the reduction of a gold salt, most commonly HAuCl4 by a variety of reducing agents of natural or synthetic origin. [27]33 AuNP are generated via a sequence of reactions. Initially small nuclei are formed, followed by their coalescence to generate bigger particles. Subsequent slow growth along with the reduction of HAuCl4 resulted in the formation of colloidal AuNP [34]. Shape and size is greatly dependent on the concentration of the precursor and the reaction conditions. The present method provides a single step procedure, where high temperature and pressure treatment has been employed for synthesizing CD, with simultaneous reduction of HAuCl4. Polar functionalities of CD render excellent water solubility as well as stabilize the metal nanoparticles [15,20,35]. Force of interaction between these two nanomaterials is presumed to be the combination of electrostatic and secondary interactions. Stability of AuNP further implies the accuracy and precision of the biosensor probe.

Again, the carboxylic functionalities of CD provide a platform for stable interaction with the enzyme, ChOx. The amino groups of ChOx can have strong bonding interaction with CD, rendering an enzyme mediated biosensor probe for selective sensing of cholesterol [36]. Scheme 1] pictorially depicts the synthetic protocol for Ch-AuCD.

A peak for AuCD was observed at around 545 nm, which implicates the presence of AuNP within the sensing probe (Fig. 1 a) [33]. Again, the peak at 254 nm confirmed the n-π* transition, due to the presence of C = O functionalities of CD. The individual UV–vis spectra are also shown in the figure for AuNP and CD. This confirmed that AuCD comprises the characteristics of both the nanomaterials. Again, a stable interaction between AuNP and CD can be predicted from the UV–vis spectrum of AuCD.

Crystallographic pattern of AuNP was analyzed by X-ray diffractogram. Only (111) basal plan of gold can be seen from the peak at around 2θ of 34.2° (Fig. 1b). The common peaks at ∼44 (200), ∼64 (220) and ∼77 (311) are not prominent in the diffractogram [33]. This may be due to the presence of CD in larger ratio as compared to Au, which might have led to a masking effect. Again, a broad peak at round 2θ of 21.2° indicates the amorphous nature of CD.

Morphology of CD and AuCD is visualized from FESEM micrographs (Fig. 2 a–d). Uniformly distributed CD are visible (at 175 kX magnification). Agglomerates of AuNP can be observed with block-like morphology. However, SEM study could not comment on the shape and size accord of the nanomaterials. TEM micrographs showed clear distribution of AuNP and CD uniformly over the grid. Amorphous nature of CD was clearly evident from the SAED pattern shown in Fig. 3 a (inset). Contrarily, the SAED pattern of the hexagonal AuNP (Fig. 3b, inset) vouched for the crystalline behaviour of the metal nanoparticles. The TEM micrographs showed that CD were formed with very small diameters (2–8 nm), in close proximity with AuNP of different shapes. Hexagonal AuNP are clearly visible from Fig. 3c, however, CD are poorly visible due to the low contrast on the substrate.
Interlayer spacing of CD was found to be 0.34 from the HRTEM images (Fig. 3d inset), which resembles most of the values reported in the literature [20].

For the detection of cholesterol, Ch-AuCD was analyzed for fluorescence emission against a range of excitation wavelengths. After detailed scrutiny, optimum excitation wavelength was fixed at 360 nm and emission spectra were recorded from 380 to 700 nm. Significant fluorescence emission was observed at 0.3 mg/mL concentration of ChOx immobilized AuCD. This concentration was used for further experiments. The absolute quantum yield was determined at 360 nm by using an integrated sphere which was found as 7.5%.

The photoluminescence behaviour of CD greatly depends upon the concentration and excitation wavelength [37]. Priyadarshini and co-workers demonstrated the minimal quenching of fluorescence intensity of CD upon addition of variable concentrations of AuNP [15]. We however optimized the concentration of the quencher; GO by recording the turn-off intensity of the Ch-AuCD probe as a whole. Formation of Ch-AuCD/GO assembly resulted in the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of the probe (Fig. 4]). This turn off intensity is attributed to the electron transfer from Ch-AuCD to GO [6]. Graphene based nanomaterials have been reported as super-quenchers for organic dyes. This is due to the non-radiative energy transfer from dye system to the π-system of graphene nanostructures [38]. We observed the turn off fluorescence intensity of Ch-AuCD with varying concentrations of GO, ranging from 5 to 55 μg/mL (Fig. 4a). With a similar approach, Wei et al. reported a K+ ion-sensor with high selectivity by establishing FRET between CD and GO [39]. Here, the decrease in fluorescence intensity maintained a linear behaviour with a regression value of 0.99 (Fig. 4b). Thus, 55 μg/mL was taken as the optimum concentration of GO, the quencher.

FRET based mechanism for biosensing has been put forwarded to achieve high sensitivity of detection [13]. After, addition of the quencher, we incorporated cholesterol with a wide range of concentrations and observed the fluorescence turn on behaviour (Fig. 5 a). We witnessed a linearity of fluorescence intensity increment with the increase in cholesterol concentration (R2 value of 0.98). However, below 10 nM concentration, addition of cholesterol did not help in regaining the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore. Thus, it implicates that the lower limit of detection of the present biosensor to be ∼10 nM.

Cholesterol preferably binds to ChOx in the Ch-AuCD system due to the well-known enzyme-substrate affinity. In presence of cholesterol, ChOx got digested, which led to the exposure of AuCD. Subsequently, AuCD assembly tends to aggregate without the presence of ChOx. Thus, electron transfer from AuCD to GO was restricted, resulting in the recovery of the fluorescence intensity [13]. Fig. 6] pictorially presents a plausible mechanism of the working principle of the Ch-AuCD biosensor probe. So, the present investigation tries to demonstrate a highly sensitive biosensor over the reported label-free biosensors [15].

For any sensing platform, selectivity is one of the top most priorities in order to validate its practical utility. Therefore, selectivity of ChOx immobilized AuCD/GO sensing platform towards cholesterol was studied in presence of different potential interferents which may be present in a real sample. The interfering effect of ascorbic acid, urea, glucose, galactose, L-Cysteine was studied towards the detection of cholesterol by using Ch-AuCD/GO sensing platform. Generally, these are some basic biomolecules which are present in human serum and may possess the capability to interfere in the cholesterol detection [15]. The interfering effect was studied by measuring the static fluorescence spectrum in presence of different analytes. Biomolecule having the interfering effect would possess the fluorescence restoration capability, while inert would leave the intensity unaltered. However, from the graph as shown in Fig. 7 , we can see that only cholesterol exhibits considerable fluorescence restoration capability. The rest of the analytes do not possess any significant effect. This can be attributed to strong and specific interaction between cholesterol and ChOx.

In Fig. 7, the Y axis shows the relative enhancement of fluorescence intensity. However, fluorescence is not completely quenched in absence of analytes. Therefore, we showed the relative enhancement of fluorescence in presence of different bio-entities taking fluorescence intensity of the blank (absence of any analyte) as reference. The overall enhancement was expressed in terms of percentage considering the enhancement of fluorescence intensity in presence of cholesterol as 100%.

Due to highly specific interaction, only cholesterol brings strong steric disturbance between AuCD and GO sheets causing the separation between both the nanostructures. As a result, FRET gets hindered and fluorescence restoration takes place. On the other hand, other bioanalytes could not approach AuCD nanohybrid due to the lake of specific bio-reorganization element and mostly occupy the free space available. Thus, observed selectivity was achieved.

A biosensor platform based on gold-carbon dot nanohybrid has been reported in the present investigation for highly sensitive detection of cholesterol, the most threatening cardio-vascular risk marker. The nanohybrid was synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method taking glucose and HAuCl4 as the precursors. AuCD was labeled by ChOx for attaining selectivity. Considerable turn off fluorescence intensity of Ch-AuCD assembly was witnessed when titrated against GO. Again, addition of cholesterol turned on the fluorescence by breaking the FRET between the donor Ch-AuCD and the acceptor GO. The lower detection limit of the present biosensor was found to be ∼10 nM. Thus, the present study put forwards a FRET based nanoswitch, operated via on-off mechanism for efficient, selective and sensitive detection of cholesterol.
All the authors have significantly contributed to the reported work. The final version of the manuscript is approved by all the authors.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Director, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat is deeply acknowledged for his kind support. SB thankfully acknowledges CSIR, India for CSIR Nehru Post Doctoral Research Fellowship (HRDG/CSIR-Nehru PDF/CS/EMR-1/01/2017). SG acknowledges SERB, DST, India for financial support through grant no. PDF/2016/003142. RK acknowledges DBT, India for financial support through Grant No. BT/PR16223/NER/95/494/2016.
